nodes.reason: Single claim: platform will not meaningfully improve reasoning

Three independent propositions: cannot resolve value-laden claims; can map them; the dispute is about goals not mechanism

Evidence illustrating one point: weighing is required, not just establishing atomics
Two separable claims: values and ethics are real generators AND no argument map can resolve them

Two independent claims: formal mapping is inefficient AND the intended user barely exists
Single argumentative claim: unsolved for 2000 years, therefore not fixable by new tool

Single main claim with supporting design details: Reasonable avoids the structure critique
Single claim: goal is to locate and make explicit the weighing, not resolve it
Evidence: demonstrates intended behavior on genuinely weighed questions
Single main claim with multiple mechanisms: values get first-class treatment
Single main claim with implementation details: grammar natively handles over-determination

Evidence: map construction and rating can be automated at low cost
Single argumentative claim: historical inference is flawed because conditions have changed
Single claim: clarifies what Reasonable does (produces shared map, not consensus)
Two related claims: rating rules measure crowd agreement and invert on bias; fixing requires scarce external anchors
Three independent empirical findings: tyranny of median retired; reputation inverts; calibration must decline
Single finding: dispersion is unreliable signal, so contestedness must be read structurally
Single claim: adoption at scale with humans remains unproven
Single overarching claim: Scott's critique concerns goals and intent, not functionality
Single argument: disagreement is over-determined and value-laden; Reasonable handles it by mapping
Single value claim: faithful mapping is worth building even if no consensus achieved
Single value claim: tool worth building only if produces agreement or mind-change
Single claim with evidence: arguments rarely hinge on false facts; tool aimed at this is misguided
Evidence: AI-risk panel illustrates weighing of theoretical vs empirical, no false fact
Single claim: adding circles past threshold makes the form counterproductive
Single claim: Reasonable designed to handle drop-false-fact-and-continue case

Compatible: model assumes wrong structure (n004) AND Reasonable avoids that via graded/structural features (n012)
Compatible: real work is magnitudes/weighing (n005) AND goal is to locate and make explicit that weighing (n013)
Compatible: values are real generators (n007) AND Reasonable gives values first-class treatment (n015)
Compatible: positions are over-determined (n008) AND grammar natively models over-determination (n016)
Compatible: mapping inefficient for arguers (n009) AND tool designed for third-party readers (n017), not arguers
Factually compatible: unsolved for 2000 years (n010) AND conditions have changed (n019) are both true; disagreement is about inference relevance
n027 (worth building if maps faithfully) and n028 (only worth if produces agreement) express contradictory criteria for tool value
Compatible: arguments rarely hinge on false facts (n029) AND Reasonable designed to handle this case (n032)
Compatible: traditional circles fail past threshold (n031) AND Reasonable uses better design (n012) avoiding that failure mode

nodes.reason: Combines three independent critiques: (1) misdescribes disagreement structure, (2) not practically usable, (3) whole endeavor doomed—joined by 'and' and 'so'
Core claim (platform succeeds) plus independent assertion (this meets skeptical objections)—second is inference that could be rejected
Separable claims: (1) whether it works depends on your standard, (2) this is value-laden, (3) can only be mapped not settled
Two linked claims: (1) disagreements don't decompose cleanly, (2) forcing them into maps misrepresents weighing as necessary—joined by 'so'
Two separable observations: (1) weighing and values are true generators, (2) positions are over-determined—joined by 'and'
Two independent claims: (1) mapping is inefficient, (2) the imagined user barely exists—joined by 'and'

Combines historical claim (millennia unsolved) with multiple modern admissions (non-production of agreement, consensus-truth problem, unreliable signal, unproven adoption)—all supporting one inference
Two linked claims: (1) these map features model weighing correctly, (2) refuted grounds being demoted while dependents survive is normal outcome—joined by 'so'
Separable prescriptions: (1) goal is to make weighing explicit with values separate, (2) unresolved verdict counts as success—joined by 'so'
Two independent defensive moves: (1) audience is third parties not live arguers, (2) low-cost authorship removes burden—joined by 'and'
Explanatory claim with evidence: (1) historical failure was due to missing conditions, (2) new conditions now exist—joined by 'since'
Three separable claims: (1) critique targets resolution, (2) defense targets representation, (3) therefore they talk past each other—final claim is inference
Two linked claims: (1) question is over-determined and value-laden, (2) therefore can't be settled through argument—joined by 'so'
Two separable claims: (1) inability to resolve values is distinct from inability to represent, (2) conceding one doesn't concede other—joined by 'so'
Single value proposition: tool worth building if represents faithfully, with 'even if no agreement' clarifying scope not adding separate claim
Single value prescription with justification: tool only worth building if changes minds or produces agreement; 'since' clause is supporting reason not independent proposition
Single claim: load-bearing work is quantifying/weighing magnitudes, not resolving premises
Single claim: many disagreements generated by values not facts
Single claim: positions held through many weak considerations not one crux
Single claim: disagreements don't decompose into tidy premises; comparative clause reinforces
Single claim: debunking one fact doesn't end disagreement
Single claim: some disputes trace entirely to weighing differences, not factual disagreement
Single evidence proposition: panel disagreement traced to weighting/priors not facts
Single claim: denser maps further miscast probabilistic weighing as necessary deduction
Single evidence proposition: even granting economic harm, verdict requires multiple weighing judgments
Evidence of one thing: examples of value-frameworks driving disagreement
Causal claim joined to inference: (1) position held through many considerations, (2) therefore single refutations don't move it—joined by 'Because'
Single claim: mapping is inefficient at mind-changing compared to ordinary persuasion
Single claim: the imagined user (rigor-seeking online arguer) barely exists
Single claim: online arguers aren't motivated to update beliefs
Two linked claims: (1) edges carry graded strength, (2) therefore grounds can partially back claims—joined by 'so'
Two linked claims: (1) disagreement is rival claims not negation, (2) therefore map needn't force binary—joined by 'so'
Single claim about mechanism: joint-support groupings model over-determination; 'mirroring' clause explains not adds
Two linked claims: (1) rewording stacks group phrasings, (2) therefore support preserved—joined by 'so'
Single claim: when ground refuted, graded map reduces support strength but doesn't delete claim
Historical claim linked to inference: (1) philosophy/rhetoric spent 2000 years without solving debate, (2) therefore problem isn't just tooling gap—joined by 'which is evidence'
Single claim: builders concede non-achievement of actual agreement under that definition of success
Single claim: consensus-truth problem persists—platform lacks reliable way to distinguish truth from crowd bias
Two linked claims: (1) rules measure crowd agreement, (2) therefore on biased majorities they reward wrong view—joined by 'so'
Two separable claims: (1) correction requires external anchors, (2) those anchors scarce on contested questions—joined by 'and'
Two linked claims: (1) dispersion is unreliable signal, (2) therefore contestedness must be inferred structurally—joined by 'so'
Single claim: adoption at scale by real communities remains unproven
Single evidence claim: demos consisted of AI agents not real communities
Single claim: success for contested questions is locating weighing and range, not determining winner
Two linked claims: (1) values get dedicated node type separated from facts, (2) therefore value cruxes mapped correctly—joined by 'so'
Single evidence claim: unresolved verdict scored highly in pandemic-origin assessment
Two claims: (1) value nodes rated by endorsement, (2) because values can't be assessed as true/false—'since' introduces independent factual claim
Single claim: intended audience is third-party readers and future deciders, not live disputants
Two linked claims: (1) mind-changing efficiency is wrong success criterion, (2) because map not built for that—'since' introduces justification that could be independent
Single claim: AI agents remove authorship cost burden
Single evidence claim: AI agents built and assessed multiple questions end-to-end
Single claim: automated low-cost authorship/rating is genuinely new capability
Single claim: accuracy-weighting mechanism is new (earlier efforts lacked it)
Single claim: blind rating before seeing others is new mechanism
Single evidence claim: tools remained unrealized not because unsolvable but because medium/incentives absent
Single evidence claim: multiple questions built and assessed by AI demonstrating low-cost at scale
Two linked claims: (1) success depends on many weak considerations (what counts as resolution, audience, conditions), (2) therefore no single consideration could settle it—joined by 'so'
Single claim: success turns on value judgment about purpose, not checkable fact
Single evidence claim: presents two competing evaluative frameworks
Three separable claims: (1) over-determined value disputes can't be settled, (2) can be located/made explicit, (3) can't be resolved—chains of inference
Single claim: defense concedes tool doesn't make parties agree
Single claim: strongest objections concern whether tool resolves disagreement
Single claim: defense claims about representation, not resolution
Single evidence claim: unresolved verdict scored high in pandemic-origin assessment
Single claim: forceful objections target resolution, not faithful representation
Single claim: under that definition of solving debate, approach concedes it doesn't achieve it
Two linked claims: (1) values get dedicated node type separate from facts, (2) therefore value disagreement represented correctly—joined by 'so'
Two claims: (1) prescription nodes rated on endorsement, (2) because values can't be true/false—'since' introduces independent claim

sets.reason: n001 claims tool is doomed; n002 claims it succeeds; n003 claims the dispute is value-laden. If n003 is true, both n001 and n002 could be correct under different value-based standards (e.g., n001 applies to changing live arguers; n002 applies to informing third parties). Members are compatible under n003's framing.

n008 claims graded maps with these features model weighing correctly; n004 claims forcing disagreements into logical maps misrepresents probabilistic weighing. These directly contradict about the same object—if n008's features work, n004's criticism fails.

n005 is descriptive: disagreement generated by weighing/values and is over-determined. n009 is prescriptive: we should make weighing explicit and accept unresolved as success. These are compatible—n009 describes a fitting strategy given n005's terrain. No contradiction between factual description and normative response.

n011 claims historical failure was due to missing conditions, which now exist. n007 claims history remains unsolved and modern admissions persist. Both factual claims can be simultaneously true—disagreement is about significance/interpretation of the same facts.

n015: tool worth building if represents faithfully (even without agreement). n016: tool only worth building if changes minds or produces agreement. These are contradictory value prescriptions—n015 claims representation sufficient; n016 claims agreement necessary. If n016 is correct, n015 is false.

sets.reason: n001/n002 oppose on binary improvement claim; n003 is meta-claim framing dispute as goal-mismatch; n001 and n003 can both hold if n003 correctly identifies the real disagreement
Challenge-answer pair: n004 describes current form presuppositions, n027 says form can be different; both true as descriptive fact (current) versus prescriptive possibility (could be)

Challenge-answer pair: n009 critiques scope (maps only settle premises), n031 redefines job (to locate/exhibit, not resolve); both can hold -- map does settle premises AND that's not the intended purpose
Challenge-answer pair: n016 says not viable for changing arguers' minds, n035 says viable for third-party audience; both true for different use contexts
Genuine rivalry on causal inference: n019 argues historical failure implies unfixability, n040 explicitly rejects this inference; cannot hold same position on whether 2000 years implies unsolvability

Challenge-answer pair: n008 critiques one design choice (adding premises), n028 says design need not make that choice (can use graded support); both true -- adding premises IS bad, but design avoids this
Genuine value conflict on worthiness: n044 asserts faithful mapping has intrinsic value, n045 asserts only mind-changing tools merit building; incompatible value judgments on same object
